settings.title: Pick One Number Before You Pick Ten
settings.excerpt: Most small teams track too much and act on too little, so we start clients with a single guiding metric.
settings.body:
When a new client shows us their dashboard, it usually has dozens of numbers on it. The problem is not data. The problem is that nothing on the screen tells the team what to do next.

We ask one question early on. If you could only watch a single number for the next quarter, what would it be? The answer forces a real conversation about what the business is trying to achieve right now.

Why one number works better than ten

A wall of metrics feels responsible, but it quietly spreads attention so thin that nothing gets acted on. When everything is being watched, nothing is really being managed. A single guiding number does the opposite. It gives a team something to rally around and a clear way to tell, week to week, whether the work is paying off.

The right number is different for every business. For one client it was repeat orders. For another it was the time between a quote going out and a yes coming back. What they had in common was that the whole team could see how their own work moved it.

How to choose yours

Choose a number you can influence weekly. A metric you cannot move within a quarter belongs on an annual review, not a Monday standup. A good guiding number tends to share a few traits.

  • The team can affect it through this week's work
  • It points clearly at the business goal right now
  • Everyone can agree on how it is measured

Once that one number is clear, the supporting metrics almost choose themselves. They stop being a list to admire and become a short set of signals that explain why the main number moved.
